perf: replace ignoredHosts map scan with typed ignoredHostSet

The old isIgnoredHost implementation iterated the entire map[string]bool
on every call to check wildcard entries (O(n) per request).  For the
common case of a few dozen ignored-host entries this was minor, but
semantically wrong — map iteration order is random and the check
interleaved wildcard and exact entries.

Introduce ignoredHostSet with two separate buckets:
  - exact:     map[string]bool — O(1) lookup via hash
  - wildcards: []string        — O(k) HasSuffix scan where k = number
                                 of wildcard entries (typically ≤ 5)

NewReverseProxy calls newIgnoredHostSet once at construction time and
stores the result; all per-request calls use ignored.contains() with
no allocation.  The public isIgnoredHost wrapper is kept for test code.

Add MTEBEvaluator for embedding model evaluation (#2409)

## Summary

Add a built-in MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) evaluator to
Olive, following the same architecture as `LMEvaluator`/`lmeval_ort.py`.

## Changes

### `olive/evaluator/olive_evaluator.py`
- New `MTEBEvaluator` class registered in the evaluator registry
- Supports three model classes: `hf` (SentenceTransformer), `ort` (ONNX
via ORT), `ortgenai` (GenAI with hidden_states)
- Auto-detects model class from handler type, including GenAI detection
via `genai_config.json`

### `olive/evaluator/mteb_ort.py` (new)
- `MTEBOnnxBase`: abstract base implementing MTEB's EncoderProtocol with
mean pooling
- `MTEBORTEvaluator`: wraps plain ONNX models via `ort.InferenceSession`
- `MTEBORTGenAIEvaluator`: wraps GenAI models using `og.Generator` +
`hidden_states` output

## Usage

```json
"evaluators": {
    "mteb": {
        "type": "MTEBEvaluator",
        "tasks": ["STS17"],
        "batch_size": 32
    }
},
"evaluator": "mteb"
```

## Testing

Tested end-to-end with Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B CPU recipe — ModelBuilder
export + MTEB STS17 evaluation on both input HF model and exported GenAI
model.

commit.message:
fix: CSP token with path component now correctly rewritten

rewriteCSPToken only stripped port suffixes to isolate the hostname,
but CSP source expressions can include a path, e.g.:

  connect-src https://api.github.com/v2/*

Before this fix, the host comparison received 'api.github.com/v2/*'
which never matched the target domain, leaving the token unchanged
and the upstream hostname leaking through the Content-Security-Policy.

Fix: strip the path component (first '/') BEFORE stripping the port,
so hostname matching works for all CSP source expression forms.

Adds two new TestRewriteCSP subtests covering token-with-path and
token-with-port-and-path.

feat: add Copy path and Show in folder to worktree context menu (#12997)

* feat: add Copy path and Show in folder to worktree context menu

Add two new context menu items for worktree nodes in the left panel:
- Copy path: copies the worktree path to clipboard (uses ClipboardUtil)
- Show in folder: opens the worktree directory in File Explorer (uses OsShellUtil)

Both items are available on all worktree nodes including the current one.
Show in folder is disabled when the directory no longer exists.
A separator visually groups these items below Open/Delete.
Reuses existing icons (CopyToClipboard, BrowseFileExplorer) and translation
patterns (Copy &path, Show &in folder) consistent with the file status list.

* fix: resolve race condition in GitlabAdapter page fetching

The ContinueWith callbacks called observer.OnNext concurrently from
thread pool threads, violating the Rx contract and causing flaky test
failures where items were lost due to concurrent List<T>.Add calls.

Fetch pages in parallel with Task.WhenAll, then process results
sequentially. Also consolidate the OnCompleted call to a single site.

commit.message:
perf+feat: cache Replacer lookup maps; add SVG body rewriting

Performance: Replacer.ToOriginal / ToAlias / *Diff methods previously
allocated and populated a new map[string]string on every call (i.e.
once per proxied request body, header, and query string).  Move the
construction to NewReplacer so lookup maps are built once and reused
across all calls.  The hot path inside ReplaceAllStringFunc now does
only a map read with no allocation.

SVG rewriting: add 'image/svg+xml' to textContentTypes so that SVG
responses undergo host masking and string replacement.  SVG is an
XML text format whose href, xlink:href, and url() values can reference
upstream hostnames that must be rewritten for the proxy to work
correctly (e.g. inline <image href="https://ctf.io/logo.png"> in a
proxied SVG asset).

commit.message:
security: SSRF guard returns 403 Forbidden instead of silent reroute

Previously, /__sd__/<evil-host>/ requests that failed the subdomain
validation check were silently rerouted to the main target — the client
received a confusing response with no indication of the block.

Now the director sets ssrfBlockedKey{} on the request context; the new
ssrfGuardTransport wrapper intercepts this before making any upstream
connection and returns a synthetic 403 response with the blocked hostname
in the body. No upstream TCP connection is made for blocked requests.

- Add ssrfBlockedKey context key type
- Add ssrfGuardTransport (wraps rt; always applied in NewReverseProxy)
- Director: set context key + clean path instead of silently rerouting
- TestSSRFBlockedViaSdPath: assert 403 status + hostname in body

  width → min(100%, calc(max-height × 640/448)) to avoid overflow on
  short/wide viewports.

  explicit height:auto !important; aspect-ratio:640/448 (set by existing
  overlay JS) then drives proportional height.

feat(ts-http-runtime): migrate to #platform/* subpath imports (#37974)

## Migrate `@typespec/ts-http-runtime` to `#platform/*` subpath imports

Replaces warp's `polyfillSuffix` mechanism with Node.js [subpath
imports](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#subpath-imports)
(`#platform/*`) for all platform-specific code in `ts-http-runtime`.

### Why

`polyfillSuffix` works by silently swapping files at build time (e.g.
`foo.ts` → `foo-browser.mts`) based on the warp target. This is
invisible to TypeScript — all platform variants share one set of type
declarations, so the compiler can't distinguish what's available on each
target. It also forces runtime environment sniffing for any logic that
needs to know its platform, which is fundamentally unreliable in hybrid
environments.

Node.js subpath imports solve both problems: each platform variant gets
its own types (caught at compile time), and the correct module is
selected by the package manager/bundler resolving `package.json`
conditions — no runtime guesswork.

Concrete benefits:

- **Type safety** — each platform variant is independently type-checked.
For example, the Node `isNodeReadableStream` returns `x is Readable`
(from `node:stream`), the browser version uses `instanceof
ReadableStream`, and RN returns `x is never`. The old approach silently
produced incorrect type narrowing on non-Node platforms.

- **Environment detection correctness** — the old `checkEnvironment.ts`
ran `typeof window`, `typeof process`, etc. on every platform, producing
wrong results in Electron, Cloudflare Workers, Deno, and React Native
(see [linked issues](#runtime-detection-issues) below). Per-platform
`env` modules use static constants that are compile-time truths.

- **Simpler testing** — the old test scripts required `dev-tool run
build-package` and `dev-tool run build-test` to manually resolve
polyfill suffixes before tests could run. With subpath imports, Vitest
resolves `#platform/*` modules natively via `resolve.conditions` — no
prebuild step needed:
  ```diff
-"test:browser": "npm run clean && dev-tool run build-package &&
dev-tool run build-test && dev-tool run test:vitest --no-test-proxy
--browser",
-"test:node": "dev-tool run build-test --no-browser-test && dev-tool run
test:vitest --no-test-proxy",
  +"test:browser": "dev-tool run test:vitest --no-test-proxy --browser",
  +"test:node": "dev-tool run test:vitest --no-test-proxy",
+"test:react-native": "dev-tool run test:vitest --no-test-proxy
--browser -- -c vitest.react-native.config.ts",
  ```

- **Less code** — where browser and RN share identical implementations
(no-ops, web APIs), a single `-web.mts` file replaces two duplicates,
following the pattern already established by `sha256-web.mts`,
`inspect-web.mts`, etc.

### What changed

**Platform modules** — platform-specific code extracted into modules
resolved via `#platform/*` entries in `package.json#imports`:

| Import | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `#platform/encoding` | UTF-8 encode/decode |
| `#platform/env` | environment constants (`isBrowser`, `isNodeLike`,
etc.) |
| `#platform/formData` | form data preparation |
| `#platform/policies` | platform-specific pipeline policies |
| `#platform/types` | platform type re-exports (`FormData`, `Blob`,
`File`, etc.) |
| `#platform/typeGuards` | `isNodeReadableStream` /
`isWebReadableStream` |

Each has a Node (`.ts`), browser (`.mts`), and react-native (`.mts`)
variant selected at build time by warp target conditions and at test
time by Vite's `resolve.conditions`.

**Config** — package-level tsconfigs moved to `config/` subdirectory,
extending shared configs from `eng/tsconfigs/`. ESLint updated to use
`config/tsconfig.lint.json` with `projectService: false`.

**Deleted** — `checkEnvironment.ts`, redundant browser/RN duplicate
files, old root-level tsconfigs (`tsconfig.src.json`,
`tsconfig.test.json`, `tsconfig.test.node.json`,
`tsconfig.browser.config.json`, `tsconfig.samples.json`,
`tsconfig.snippets.json`).

### Runtime detection issues

The migration structurally eliminates a class of recurring bugs caused
by runtime environment sniffing:

**Electron** — both `isBrowser` and `isNodeLike` true; bundler served
browser files that threw on Node-only policies:
- #12983, #14025, #21110, #30123

**Cloudflare / Deno / edge** — `nodejs_compat` flags set
`process.versions.node`, triggering Node-only policies the runtime

perf: add minLen prefilter to @pm operator (#1601)

* perf: add minLen prefilter to @pm operator

Compute the length of the shortest pattern at operator init time and
skip the Aho-Corasick automaton entirely when the input is shorter —
no pattern can possibly match.

This mirrors the minMatchLength prefilter already used by @rx. For
@pm the computation is trivial (min of literal lengths) vs @rx which
walks the regexp AST.

Applies to all @pm variants: pm, pmFromFile, pmFromDataset.

Micro-benchmark (10 patterns, Apple M2):
  below_minLen:    2ns/op, 0 allocs  (was ~98ns with Aho-Corasick)
  at_minLen:      98ns/op, 4 allocs  (unchanged — hits automaton)

CRS benchmark (benchstat, count=6):
  Memory:      -3.04% geomean (p=0.015)
  Allocations: -1.87% geomean (p=0.002)
  Time:        -2.92% geomean

* fix: minPatternLen returns 0 on empty pattern to disable incorrect short-circuit

* connect persona, competitor, and enhancement modules

Enhancement Module
What it does: Acts as the main controller. It pulls data from all other modules and feeds it into the LangGraph AI to write the final, enhanced post.
Smart Feature: If any data is missing (like trends or competitor posts), it can automatically run the other scripts to fetch what it needs.

Persona Module (The Brand Voice)
What it does: Looks at your past Facebook posts to figure out how your brand sounds.
Smart Feature: It uses AI to generate 3 distinct "Persona Options" (e.g., friendly, professional, edgy) for you to choose from, saving it as your official brand voice.

Competitors Scraper Module (The Spy)
What it does: Finds businesses similar to yours, checks if they are actual competitors, and scrapes their top-performing posts.
Smart Feature: It translates and cleans up competitor posts so the AI can learn what kind of content gets the most engagement in your niche

commit.message:
fix: prevent ghost articles from RSS re-publishing

Root cause: RSS feeds (Google Developers Blog etc) update article timestamps
when editing, causing the same article to pass the 72h cutoff filter on
every pipeline run. 'What's new in TensorFlow 2.21' stayed in feed for a week.

Fix: persistent seen_ids.json tracks all article IDs across runs.
- New articles skip if ID already seen (14-day retention)
- Prevents 'ghost articles' from re-entering the pipeline
- seen_ids auto-prunes entries older than 14 days
